title: Lady Chatterley's Lover
description: A novel of desire, class, marriage, and bodily candor that became a defining obscenity case.

cover.alt: Lady Chatterley's Lover cover thumbnail from Wikipedia

overview.englishDescription: A novel of desire, class, marriage, and bodily candor that became a defining obscenity case.
banningParagraphs: Lady Chatterley's Lover entered censorship debates as a novel associated with sexuality, class, and marriage. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around obscenity and sexual explicitness.

The earliest event currently captured here is 1928-1960 in United Kingdom, where British obscenity authorities banned unexpurgated editions. The novel was treated as obscene until its famous court case helped shift standards. This is one of the canonical legal turning points in English-language obscenity law.
This entry is still incomplete: more jurisdictions, court orders, and translated justifications should be added over time.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: Literature Suppressed on Sexual Grounds
counterReadings.author: Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: Surveys the legal and moral language used to suppress books as obscene.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for seeing how obscenity law and censorship habits changed over time.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.
